---
title: "Hearts & Flowers — item structure (start/stay/switch) and 2PL vs Rasch"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.5
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Question

Hearts & Flowers (inhibitory control / cognitive flexibility) has been through
many scoring iterations. The most recent view: the nuanced item structure —
distinguishing **start / stay / switch** trials within each block, and using a
**2PL** rather than a Rasch model — fits better. Here we check that on
corrected (v1.2) data, and look at whether the structure replicates across
sites (DE looked possibly noisier than CO).

Item structure (`item_uid = {block}_{stim}_{type}`):

- **block** — `hearts` (congruent only), `flowers` (incongruent only),
  `heartsflowers` (mixed)
- **type** — `start` (first trial of a block), `stay` (rule repeats),
  `switch` (rule changes; mixed block only)

```{r setup}
source(here::here("common.R"))
library(mirt)
ip <- load_item_parameters() |>
  filter(task_id == "hearts-and-flowers", subset == "all_items") |>
  mutate(block = str_extract(item_uid, "^(heartsflowers|hearts|flowers)"),
         type  = factor(str_extract(item_uid, "start|stay|switch"),
                        levels = c("start","stay","switch")),
         stim  = str_extract(item_uid, "heart|flower"))
```

# 1. Item difficulty and discrimination

H&F is scored with a **2PL** model, so each item has both a difficulty and a
discrimination. The discrimination is where the action is.

```{r params-table}
ip |> select(item_uid, block, type, difficulty, discrimination, n_responses) |>
  arrange(block, type) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2, caption = "H&F 2PL item parameters")
```

```{r param-plot, fig.height=5}
ip |>
  ggplot(aes(x = difficulty, y = discrimination, color = type, shape = block)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = str_remove(item_uid, "_(heart|flower)")),
                           size = 2.6, show.legend = FALSE, max.overlaps = 20) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "Difficulty (logits)", y = "Discrimination (2PL a)",
       title = "H&F items: difficulty and discrimination by trial type",
       subtitle = "Switch items hardest; start trials discriminate least")
```

```{r by-type}
ip |> group_by(type) |>
  summarise(n = n(), mean_difficulty = mean(difficulty),
            mean_discrimination = mean(discrimination), .groups = "drop") |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2, caption = "By trial type")
```

Two clear patterns:

- **Difficulty:** pure blocks (hearts/flowers) are easy; the **mixed block** is
  harder, and **switch** trials are the hardest of all (the switch cost). This
  is the expected executive-function signature.
- **Discrimination:** **start trials discriminate least** (a ≈ 1.75) vs. stay
  (≈ 2.28) and switch (≈ 2.32). Start trials are the first of a block — warm-up,
  noisier — so they carry less ability information. Forcing them to share a
  single discrimination (Rasch) misfits; giving them their own item label under
  a 2PL captures it.

# 2. 2PL vs Rasch

```{r irt-fit, cache=TRUE}
tr <- load_levante_trials(task_ids = "hearts-and-flowers") |>
  filter(dataset %in% c("pilot_mpieva_de_main", "pilot_uniandes_co_bogota")) |>
  mutate(correct = as.numeric(correct))
wide <- tr |> distinct(run_id, item_uid, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  select(run_id, item_uid, correct) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = item_uid, values_from = correct) |>
  select(-run_id) |> as.matrix()
wide <- wide[, colMeans(!is.na(wide)) > 0.2]
wide <- wide[rowSums(!is.na(wide)) > 0, ]

m_rasch <- mirt(wide, 1, itemtype = "Rasch", verbose = FALSE)
m_2pl   <- mirt(wide, 1, itemtype = "2PL",   verbose = FALSE)
anova(m_rasch, m_2pl) |> as.data.frame() |>
  select(AIC, BIC, logLik, X2, df, p) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 2, caption = "Rasch vs 2PL (pooled DE+CO)")
```

The 2PL is preferred by the likelihood-ratio test (χ² ≈ 50, df = 9, p < .001)
and AIC. (BIC is close, since the extra discrimination parameters are penalised
heavily, but the LR test and the clear discrimination heterogeneity above favor
2PL.) This confirms the current scoring choice: **start trials genuinely
discriminate differently, and a 2PL is the right model.**

# 3. Does the structure replicate across sites?

```{r site-acc, fig.height=5}
acc <- tr |> group_by(dataset_label, item_uid) |>
  summarise(acc = mean(correct, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(ip |> select(item_uid, type, block), by = "item_uid") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = dataset_label, values_from = acc)

acc |>
  ggplot(aes(x = `Colombia — Bogotá`, y = `Germany — Leipzig`, color = type)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, lty = 3, color = "grey50") +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = "Item accuracy — Bogotá", y = "Item accuracy — Leipzig",
       title = "H&F item accuracy across sites",
       subtitle = glue::glue("r = {round(cor(acc$`Colombia — Bogotá`, acc$`Germany — Leipzig`, use='complete.obs'),2)}; consistent ordering, Bogotá slightly higher"))
```

Item accuracies correlate **r ≈ 0.94** across sites — the difficulty structure
replicates. Here Bogotá is slightly *higher*-accuracy than Leipzig (the
opposite direction from Memory, where Leipzig was higher), so there is no
general "Leipzig is noisier" effect: each task's site-level offset reflects
sample/age, not measurement noise.

# Verdict

- The **start / stay / switch** structure is psychometrically real: switch
  trials are hardest (switch cost), and **start trials discriminate least**
  (warm-up noise).
- **2PL fits better than Rasch** (LR + AIC), driven by that discrimination
  heterogeneity — the current scoring is right to model it.
- Structure replicates across sites (r ≈ 0.94); site differences are level, not
  noise.
- H&F looks **usable on corrected data**; the main subtlety to carry forward is
  that start trials are low-information (one could down-weight or drop them for
  a tighter ability estimate, but the 2PL already handles this).

```{r save}
write_rds(ip, here::here("data", "hf_item_parameters.rds"))
```
